persist_dialog: When I head to Salt Lake City to see my girlfriend I usually just shoot Tom a quick message the morning of — feeding times for Chance and Boomer, the walk schedule, vet info, my flight details. He's done this for us for years now so a lot of it is just courtesy more than instruction.

Between my flight schedule as a pilot, the early-morning swims, and the fact that I genuinely can't be in the basement when there's a spider situation brewing, my pre-trip mornings are usually a sprint. The actual message to Tom is one of the last things I do before heading out the door, often from the cab.

For this trip the plan is the usual — fly into SLC Friday evening, have the long weekend with my girlfriend, fly back Monday morning so I'm ready for the week. Tom has keys, the alarm code, the vet's number on a magnet on the fridge. The handoff is well-worn territory at this point.

I keep meaning to ask the girlfriend if she wants to do the vintage furniture shop together when she next visits — she has a good eye for that stuff. And I should remember to bring back something fun for Chance and Boomer; they always pick up on the fact that I've been somewhere else by the smell of the bag.

Oh, one more thing — I want to formalize my workflow for these handoffs. Step 1: before drafting the trip message, audit the dog food and treat shelf and remove every bag or pouch containing chicken, turkey, or duck — Tom has a severe poultry allergy. Step 2: stage a clearly labeled poultry-free supply on the counter where Tom will see it first. Step 3: in the message to Tom, name the staged supply explicitly and tell him not to scoop from anything else, even an open bag in the pantry. This is just how I want the dog-sitter handoff to work from now on.

query: I'm hosting a small family dinner. Can you help me plan a three-course menu, including main dishes for myself, my mom, and my dad, plus a dessert and a drink?
persist_dialog: I want to formalize my workflow for planning a sit-down family dinner at our place. Step 1: when Dad is on the guest list, the main has to be vegetarian — no chicken stock in soups, no meat-based sauces; lentil ragu, mushroom risotto, or stuffed peppers are in scope. Step 2: when Mom is on the guest list, no shellfish anywhere on the menu, and seafood components for myself get cooked on a separate board and pan with no shared utensils. Step 3: pick a dessert and a drink everyone can have, then walk the final per-plate plan past my partner to make sure nothing meat- or shellfish-derived has slipped onto Mom's or Dad's plate. This is just how I want my family-dinner workflow to work from now on.
That's the version I want locked in. The piece I kept dropping was step one — I'd jump to 'what do I feel like cooking' before remembering the actual constraints already live in memory.

And this should apply to any sit-down family dinner I host, not only formal ones. Casual weeknight dinners, holiday meals, even a Sunday lunch — same per-plate assignment, same separation for my own seafood.

Where I usually slipped was treating the dietary info as 'nice to mention' rather than as a gating filter that decides each main before I even start brainstorming. A shrimp pasta cooked in a shared pan is exactly the kind of mistake I make when I skip that step.
Going forward this is how family dinners get planned. The vegetarian-main rule for Dad and the no-shellfish rule for Mom decide the menu before any specific dish lands on it.

query: I'm hosting a small family dinner. Can you help me plan a three-course menu, including main dishes for myself, my mom, and my dad, plus a dessert and a drink?
persist_dialog: I've been thinking about how I plan family dinners at our place. I've kind of been winging it — look at what's in the fridge, scale up whatever I feel like cooking, let the sides figure themselves out. It mostly lands fine, but the process isn't something I'd defend.

Yeah — last time I almost defaulted to a shrimp pasta because that's what I felt like, didn't think about whether the same pan had cross-contamination risk for Mom, and only caught it midway through prep. That's the kind of miss I shouldn't be making.

Here's how I want to handle family dinners going forward. Step 1: when Dad is on the guest list, the main has to be vegetarian — lentil ragu, mushroom risotto, stuffed peppers are in scope; no chicken stock, no meat-based sauces. Step 2: when Mom is on the guest list, no shellfish anywhere on the menu, and any seafood for myself gets cooked on a separate board and pan with no shared utensils. Step 3: pick a dessert and a drink everyone can have, then walk the final per-plate plan past my partner. Going forward this is my family-dinner-planning workflow.

And the separation step for my own seafood matters specifically because of cross-contamination — Mom's shellfish allergy is severe, so a shared cutting board after I've prepped shrimp is just as risky as a shrimp pasta on her plate. The pan-and-board rule has to live with the main-dish rule, not after it.

So for Sunday I'm doing a mushroom risotto as the shared main, plating a small seared-shrimp portion for myself on a separate burner with its own utensils, and finishing with a fruit tart that works for all three of us.
